elements.sent-2411-001: Evidence has emerged for a stochastic signal correlated among 67 pulsars within the 15-year pulsar-timing data set compiled by the NANOGrav collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-002: Similar signals have been found in data from the European, Indian, Parkes, and Chinese PTAs.
elements.sent-2411-003: This signal has been interpreted as indicative of the presence of a nanohertz stochastic gravitational wave background.
elements.sent-2411-004: To explore the internal consistency of this result we investigate how the recovered signal strength changes as we remove the pulsars one by one from the data set.
elements.sent-2411-005: We calculate the signal strength using the (noise-marginalized) optimal statistic, a frequentist metric designed to measure correlated excess power in the residuals of the arrival times of the radio pulses.
elements.sent-2411-006: We identify several features emerging from this analysis that were initially unexpected.
elements.sent-2411-007: The significance of these features, however, can only be assessed by comparing the real data to synthetic data sets.
elements.sent-2411-008: After conducting identical analyses on simulated data sets, we do not find anything inconsistent with the presence of a stochastic gravitational wave background in the NANOGrav 15-year data.
elements.sent-2411-009: The methodologies developed here can offer additional tools for application to future, more sensitive data sets.
elements.sent-2411-010: While this analysis provides an internal consistency check of the NANOGrav results, it does not eliminate the necessity for additional investigations that could identify potential systematics or uncover unmodeled physical phenomena in the data.
elements.sent-2411-011: Pulsar timing arrays (PTAs) can be used to detect gravitational waves (GWs) in the nanohertz frequency band by examining the timing residuals in a network of millisecond pulsars (MSPs) \citep{1978SvA....22...36S, 1979ApJ...234.1100D, 1990ApJ...361..300F .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The timing residuals induced by a Gaussian and isotropic stochastic background of GWs in different pulsars are correlated across the sky in a characteristic way \citep[described by the Hellings-Downs (HD) curve;][]{1983ApJ...265L..39H which allows them to be distinguished from other mismodelled processes.}
elements.sent-2411-013: It is expected that inspiralling supermassive black holes binaries (SMBHB), with masses in the range $\sim$$10^8 \,--\, 10^{10}\,M_\odot$ and redshifts $z \lesssim 2$, will produce a stochastic GW background (SGWB) in this frequency band \citep{2003ApJ...583..616J, 2004ApJ...611..623S, 2008MNRAS.390..192S, 2014ApJ...789..156M , although individual sources and cosmological backgrounds may also be present with comparable amplitudes.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, several PTA collaborations have reported the results of the analyses of their most recent data sets showing the first emerging evidence for a spatially correlated process in the data.
elements.sent-2411-015: These results came from analysing the NANOGrav 15-year data set \citep[NG15;][]{2023ApJ...951L...8A , the second data release from the European PTA \citep[EPTA2;][]{2023A A...678A..50E which includes the first data release from the Indian PTA \citep[InPTA1;][]{2018JApA...39...51J, 2023A A...678A..50E , the third data release from the Parkes PTA \citep[PPTA3;][]{2023ApJ...951L...6R and the first data release from the Chinese PTA \cite[CPTA1;][]{2023RAA....23g5024X .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In the near future, increased sensitivity is expected to be achieved using the forthcoming third data release from the International PTA (IPTA3) which will combine many of the aforementioned data sets, data from new observatories such as MeerKAT \citep[which has already made its first data release, MPTA1;][]{2023MNRAS.519.3976M and eventually the Square Kilometre Array \citep[SKA;][]{2015aska.confE..37J .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite the growing evidence for a stochastic gravitational-wave background (SGWB) there are good reasons to be cautious: tension with previous upper limits \citep[][]{2023ApJ...951L...6R ; hints of unexpected angular correlation in some data sets \citep[EPTA and NG15 monopole;][]{2023A A...678A..50E, 2023ApJ...951L...8A ; differing signal characteristics between the published data sets and even from different time slices within single data sets \citep[][]{2023A A...678A..50E, 2023ApJ...951L...6R .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Given the importance of any detection claim, it is necessary to verify the internal consistency of any data modeling \citep[e.g.,][]{2024arXiv240720510A , especially for data sets that we intend to combine with others in further analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The objective of this paper is to devise tools to carry out such internal consistency checks.
elements.sent-2411-020: This work does not, however, negate the need for additional studies aimed at detecting potential systematics and identifying any unmodeled physical phenomena in the data.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper we investigate the NANOGrav 15-year data set (NG15 hereafter).
elements.sent-2411-022: This data set is the product of a 67-pulsar PTA (those with a timing baseline $\geq$ 3 years), the most pulsars in any published PTA search currently.
elements.sent-2411-023: In the future, we plan to apply the methods developed here to other data sets, including those under construction within the IPTA.
elements.sent-2411-024: The NANOGrav collaboration recently published evidence for a SGWB in the 15-year data set \citep[NG15;][]{2023ApJ...951L...8A .}
elements.sent-2411-025: To deepen our understanding of the findings, we have developed an analysis pipeline that calculates how the recovered signal strength changes as pulsars are cumulatively removed from the PTA.
elements.sent-2411-026: Because our analysis requires the signal strength to be calculated thousands of times, we employ the noise marginalized optimal statistic, a computationally inexpensive frequentist detection metric, in our assessment of any signal.
elements.sent-2411-027: As well as a signal with the HD spatial correlations expected of a SGWB, we also searched for signals with monopole and dipole spatial correlations.
elements.sent-2411-028: A priori we anticipated that the recovered signal strength would exhibit a somewhat gradual decline as pulsars were removed from the array.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, the actual results show abrupt drops and even increases that were not anticipated.
elements.sent-2411-030: In order to quantify how unusual these features were, we designed metrics to quantify them and then compared the data to 100 realizations of simulated PTAs with a SGWB injected and with noise properties based on NG15.
elements.sent-2411-031: We find that the results of our pulsar-removal analysis of NG15 are consistent with the simulated data sets and, therefore, with a SGWB signal.
elements.sent-2411-032: It is still crucial, however, to pursue further studies that could expose underlying systematics or unmodeled physical influences in the data.
elements.sent-2411-033: When using PTAs to search for a SGWB, multiple timing noise sources vary over timescales similar to the GW signal (e.g., intrinsic pulsar noise, interstellar-medium–induced radio-frequency-dependent fluctuations, and timing-model errors).
elements.sent-2411-034: To disentangle signal from noise, a joint estimate of both can be calculated using a Bayesian framework.
elements.sent-2411-035: By performing a fully Bayesian GW search, the analysis considers the relationships between parameters, provides a more nuanced understanding of the underlying astrophysical processes and incorporates all sources of uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-036: However, a fully Bayesian analysis that incorporates HD spatial correlations requires substantial computing resources.
elements.sent-2411-037: Therefore, a useful complement to the Bayesian framework is the computationally inexpensive frequentist detection metric known as the optimal statistic \citep[OS;][]{PhysRevD.79.084030 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The vector of timing residuals in each pulsar is denoted $\delta \mathbf{t _a$, where the index $a$ labels the different pulsars in the array.}
elements.sent-2411-039: When looking for a stochastic signal process that is spatially-correlated between different pulsars, the cross-covariance between two pulsars ($a\neq b$) is used; \begin{equation}\label{eq:residuals} \rho_{ab} = \left< \delta \mathbf{t}_a \delta \mathbf{t}_b \right>.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: The OS is a frequentist estimator for the amplitude of the correlated (i.e.,\ signal) process.
elements.sent-2411-041: The optimal amplitude statistic, $\hat{A}$, is defined as the value of the amplitude parameter $A$ that minimizes the quantity \begin{equation}\label{eq:OS} \chi^2 = \sum_{a, b<a} \left(\frac{\rho_{ab}-A^2\Gamma_{ab}}{\sigma_{ab}}\right)^2\,,\end{equation} where $\sigma_{ab}$ are the uncertainties in the cross-correlations and where $\Gamma_{ab}$ is the overlap reduction function (ORF; quantifies the degree of correlation between pulsars as a function of their positions on the sky.) Under the assumptions that the noise is Gaussian and is much greater than the signal it can be shown that this statistic is optimal in the sense that it maximises the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N).
elements.sent-2411-042: Note that the autocorrelation terms involving $\rho_{aa}$ are intentionally omitted from the summation in this definition.
elements.sent-2411-043: The OS defined above assumes a known ORF for a specific spatial correlation.
elements.sent-2411-044: However, in addition to the HD spatial correlations induced by a SGWB, other sources of spatially correlated timing residuals are possible.
elements.sent-2411-045: For example, clock errors can lead to monopole spatial correlations \citep[]{2012MNRAS.427.2780H and uncertainty in the position of the solar system barycenter can produce dipole spatial correlations \citep[]{2010ApJ...720L.201C between the pulsars.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The OS can be generalized to allow for a mixture of different possible spatial correlations \citep{2023PhRvD.108l4081S .}
elements.sent-2411-047: These multiple-component optimal statistic (MCOS) amplitudes, $A^\alpha$, are now the values that minimize \begin{equation}\label{eq:MCOS} \chi^2 = \sum_{a, b<a} \left(\frac{\rho_{ab}-\sum_\alpha A_\alpha^2\Gamma^\alpha_{ab}}{\sigma_{ab}}\right)^2,\end{equation} where $\Gamma^\alpha$ is the ORF for each component.
elements.sent-2411-048: Three different spatial correlations are considered here: a monopole correlation, a dipole correlation and the HD correlation which is predominantly quadrupolar, i.e.,\ $\alpha\in\{\mathrm{Monopole ,\mathrm{Dipole ,\mathrm{HD}\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The ORFs for these spatial correlations are respectively \begin{equation}\Gamma^{\mathrm{Monopole}}_{ab} = 1, \label{eq:monopoleORF} \\\end{equation} \begin{equation}\Gamma^{\mathrm{Dipole}}_{ab} = \cos\xi_{ab},\quad \mathrm{and} \label{eq:dipoleORF} \\\end{equation} \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Gamma^{\mathrm{HD}}_{ab} = \frac{\delta_{ab}}{2} + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{(1-\cos\xi_{ab})}{4} \left( \frac{1}{2} \right. \left. - 3 \log\left[\frac{1-\cos\xi_{ab}}{2}\right] \right), \end{split} \label{eq:HDORF}\end{equation} where $\xi_{ab}$ is the angle on the sky between pulsar $a$ and pulsar $b$.
elements.sent-2411-050: In this paper, both the single-component (denoted OS; see Eq.~\ref{eq:OS ) and multiple-component (denoted MCOS; see Eq.~\ref{eq:MCOS ) are used.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Single-component OS analyses are performed one-at-a-time using each of the three ORFs in Eqs.~\ref{eq:monopoleORF to \ref{eq:HDORF .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Whenever the MCOS is used, it includes all three ORFs simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-053: The OS and the MCOS are computationally cheap to evaluate (for a typical PTA data set they can be evaluated in $\approx 0.5 \; \mathrm{seconds )$.}
elements.sent-2411-054: This characteristic makes them ideal for iterative analyses and facilitates the comprehensive exploration of a range of scenarios, such as different combinations of pulsars and spatial correlations and also when marginalizing over pulsar noise parameters (see Section \ref{subsec:nmos ).}
elements.sent-2411-055: In cases where pulsars exhibit significant red noise, the OS yields biased results due to the strong covariance between individual red noise parameters and the amplitude of the SGWB.
elements.sent-2411-056: This bias can be mitigated by marginalizing over the individual pulsars’ red noise parameters using the posterior probability distributions from a Bayesian analysis of all PTA pulsars.
elements.sent-2411-057: This Bayesian step includes a SGWB red noise model that has a spectrum common to all pulsars, but which is agnostic regarding any spatial correlations between pulsars.
elements.sent-2411-058: This step must not be confused with a Bayesian analysis complete with spatial correlations, which is a much more computationally expensive process \citep[]{2018PhRvD..98d4003V .}
elements.sent-2411-059: In practise, each time the OS is calculated, the pulsars' individual red noise parameters are drawn from the posterior distributions of the spatially uncorrelated Bayesian analysis; we calculate the OS 10,000 times, resulting in a distribution from which an average OS can be calculated.
elements.sent-2411-060: This method more accurately determines the strength of a signal embedded in the pulsar timing residuals than a single OS calculation and is many orders of magnitude faster than performing a Bayesian analysis complete with spatial correlations.
elements.sent-2411-061: We, therefore, use the noise-marginalized OS (NMOS) throughout this work.
elements.sent-2411-062: As described in Section~\ref{subsec:nmos , to calculate the NMOS we require the posterior probability distributions that result from a spatially uncorrelated Bayesian analysis of the pulsar data, which ignores spatial correlations, and we briefly describe that analysis here.}
elements.sent-2411-063: When looking for the effects of gravitational waves, the pulsar data of interest are the timing residuals, which represent the deviations between observed and predicted pulse arrival times.
elements.sent-2411-064: For each pulsar we use the NG15 pulse times of arrival (TOAs) and the NG15 noise models to compute the timing residuals.
elements.sent-2411-065: We assume that the timing residuals of the pulsars are produced by various physical processes characterized by variable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-066: Bayes' theorem is then employed to deduce the probability distribution of these parameters of interest.
elements.sent-2411-067: Under the assumption of accurate noise modeling for each pulsar, the physical processes assumed to give rise to systematic trends seen in a pulsar's timing residuals are:.
elements.sent-2411-068: The SGWB will imprint a common spectrum on all pulsar timing residuals.
elements.sent-2411-069: We model this SGWB spectrum as a $-2/3$ characteristic-strain power law, translating to a $-13/3$ power law for timing residuals.
elements.sent-2411-070: This power law is expected if the background is due to the incoherent superposition of radiation from SMBHBs in circular orbit in-spiraling due to loss of energy and angular momentum produced by GW emission ~\citep{2001astro.ph..8028P .}
elements.sent-2411-071: The amplitude of the SGWB spectrum is left as a free parameter and we assumed a log-uniform prior probability distribution between $10^{-18}$ and $10^{-11}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Suggested interpretations of pulsar red noise include intrinsic changes in the spindown rate of the pulsar \citep[]{1994ApJ...428..713K , interstellar propagation effects \citep[]{1984Natur.307..527A, 1990ARA A..28..561R, 1994ApJ...428..713K, 1995A A...296..169C , and the presence of a circumpulsar asteroid belt \citep[]{2013ApJ...766....5S .}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The red noise for each pulsar is also modeled as a power law.
elements.sent-2411-074: The amplitude and the slope of the spectrum are both free parameters.
elements.sent-2411-075: We assumed a log-uniform prior probability distribution between $10^{-20}$ and $10^{-11}$ for the amplitude of the red noise and a uniform prior probability distribution between 0 and 7 for its slope.
elements.sent-2411-076: We use Markov chain Monte Carlo techniques to sample randomly from the joint posterior distribution of our model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-077: Runs were performed using the PTMCMC sampler \citep{2017zndo...1037579E .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Each time a pulsar is removed from the PTA (as described in the next section) this Bayesian analysis step is completed anew with fewer pulsars.
elements.sent-2411-079: The red noise parameters of each pulsar subarray are then drawn from the Bayesian posterior distributions as described in Section \ref{subsec:nmos .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Our aim is to use the above methods to quickly search for a correlated signal in the timing residuals of a pulsar timing array.
elements.sent-2411-081: Any signal detected will vary as a function of the number of pulsars in the array.
elements.sent-2411-082: One by one we eliminate pulsars from the full array and observe how the recovered signal is affected.
elements.sent-2411-083: The order in which we removed pulsars is based on their noise levels, specifically the weighted root-mean-square of epoch-averaged post-fit timing residuals after whitening (i.e., any red-noise contribution is removed) as listed in Table 6 of \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...9A .\footnote{For PSR~B1937+21, the value listed in Table 6 of \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...9A is incorrect.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This was discovered in the latter stages of this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-085: As a result, PSR~B1937+21 appears at a position in the pulsar-removal ordering that reflects the value quoted in the \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...9A table.}
elements.sent-2411-086: We implemented two distinct exclusion methodologies for the NG15 pulsars: one where the least-noisy pulsars were excluded first, and another where the most-noisy pulsars were excluded first.
elements.sent-2411-087: A priori we anticipate that the removal of the least-noisy pulsars will have the most pronounced impact on the signals recovered from the timing residuals.
elements.sent-2411-088: It is for this reason that we prioritize the scenario in which the least-noisy pulsars are removed first.
elements.sent-2411-089: The case where we remove the most-noisy pulsars first is discussed in the appendix of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-090: We begin by calculating the signal found in the timing residuals of the full NG15 timing array comprised of 67 pulsars.
elements.sent-2411-091: The results from both the single-component (SC) NMOS and the multiple-component (MC) NMOS analyses are shown in Table~\ref{tab:allpulsars and Figure~\ref{fig:full_array_stats , and were calculated for monopole, dipole and HD spatial correlations.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Both our SCNMOS and MCNMOS results for the full array are consistent with those of NG15 (Figure 4 and Table 2 in \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...8A respectively).}
elements.sent-2411-093: As we are using the full array in this case, this also permits us to compare our results with the fully Bayesian analysis which includes HD spatial correlations in the \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...8A paper.}

elements.sent-2411-105: As the MCOS more accurately recovers spatially correlated signals than the OS \citep[][]{2023PhRvD.108l4081S , we have identified four features of interest in the MCNMOS pulsar-removal plots of Figure~\ref{fig:NG15_MC_bestfirst .}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The features are described and quantified in the following and are highlighted in Figure~\ref{fig:all_simulations .}
elements.sent-2411-107: Although we focused on these four features, it is important to note that various others could have been selected; we primarily chose these particular features based on notable characteristics observed in the plotted data.
elements.sent-2411-108: Feature (I): Initial S/N for the HD spatial correlations.
elements.sent-2411-109: The median S/N of the signal with HD spatial correlations when all 67 NANOGrav pulsars are included in the pulsar timing array is $\sim$2.8.
elements.sent-2411-110: We see a value close to this in Table 2 of \cite{2023ApJ...951L...8A .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Feature (II): S/N drop after removal of one pulsar for the HD spatial correlations.
elements.sent-2411-112: When we remove the least-noisy pulsars first, a S/N drop from $\sim$2.8 to $\sim$2.0 is seen in the HD spatial correlations plot when the first pulsar PSR~J1909$-$3744 is removed.
elements.sent-2411-113: This drop of 29\% after the removal of only one pulsar from the PTA appears notably large.
elements.sent-2411-114: In the highly idealized case where all pulsars are identical, the S/N is expected to scale linearly with the number of pulsars in the array ~\citep[see e.g., Equation 24 from][]{2013CQGra..30v4015S ; a 29\% drop is clearly much larger than this naive approximation would predict.}
elements.sent-2411-115: However, PSR~J1909$-$3744 is the most precisely timed pulsar and so its removal would be expected to produce a larger drop in S/N compared to other pulsars.
elements.sent-2411-116: Specifically, the weighted root-mean-square of epoch-averaged post-fit timing residuals after whitening for PSR~J1909$-$3744 is 0.066 $\mu$s \citep{2023ApJ...951L...9A , which is $\sim$43 times smaller than the highest value in NG15.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Nevertheless, the magnitude of the drop still seems surprising for a single pulsar removal and its significance will be quantified later in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-118: Feature (III): Sharp Increase in S/N for HD spatial correlations.
elements.sent-2411-119: When 33 pulsars have been removed in the order of increasing noise, the S/N of the HD spatial correlation is $\sim$-0.1.
elements.sent-2411-120: On the removal of the 34th pulsar (PSR~B1855$+$09) the S/N for the PTA consisting of the remaining 33 pulsars rises to $\sim$1.4.
elements.sent-2411-121: A priori the S/N would be expected to decrease when a pulsar is removed.
elements.sent-2411-122: It is surprising, therefore, that the removal of a single pulsar could produce such a significant S/N increase.
elements.sent-2411-123: Feature (IV): Sharp Increase in S/N for dipole spatial correlations.
elements.sent-2411-124: The S/N of the dipole spatial correlation jumps from $\sim$1 to $\sim$3 when the 37th pulsar (PSR~J2317+1439) is removed in the order of increasing noise.
elements.sent-2411-125: In order to evaluate the significance of these four features and to determine whether they indicate some unmodelled aspect of the NG15 data set, we repeat the MCNMOS calculations described in this paper on 100 simulated data sets with properties statistically akin to those of NG15.
elements.sent-2411-126: The analysis of the real and the simulated data sets are compared in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-127: To assess the peculiarity of the prominent features we see in our MCNMOS analysis of NG15, we make a comparison with an ensemble of simulated data sets that share the statistical properties of NG15.
elements.sent-2411-128: The simulated data were generated in \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...8A following \citet{2021ApJ...911L..34P .}}
elements.sent-2411-129: We note that the construction of these simulated data sets is central to the conclusions of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-130: Comprehensive details on their construction can be found in these previous references, but to summarize, each of the 100 simulated data sets share the same pulsars, timing cadence and noise parameters as NG15 and also include a spatially correlated power-law SGWB with power-law amplitude $2.7\times10^{-15}$ and spectral index $-13/3$.
elements.sent-2411-131: The data sets differ only in their random noise realizations.
elements.sent-2411-132: For each realization, we calculated the MCNMOS and removed pulsars one by one as in the analysis of NG15.
elements.sent-2411-133: By performing the same analysis on simulated data sets we are able to determine the expected behavior of the MCNMOS as pulsars are steadily removed.
elements.sent-2411-134: By comparing the results from the NG15 data with those from the ensemble of simulated data it is possible to assign a significance to the unexpected features identified in Section~\ref{subsec:MC .}
elements.sent-2411-135: Fig.~\ref{fig:sim_MC_bestfirst shows the results for a single representative simulated data set with the least-noisy pulsars removed first; this can be compared to the equivalent plot from the NG15 data set, shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:NG15_MC_bestfirst .}}
elements.sent-2411-136: To quantify the features discussed in Section~\ref{subsec:MC we define three metrics:.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The S/N with all 67 pulsars in the array.
elements.sent-2411-138: The decrease in S/N after the first pulsar (PSR~J1909$-$3744) is removed.
elements.sent-2411-139: The largest increase in S/N produced by the removal of any one pulsar.
elements.sent-2411-140: The metrics focus on S/N because each of our four features in Section~\ref{subsec:MC appears in S/N analyses.}

elements.sent-2411-153: We conducted the removal of pulsars from the array in two distinct sequences, acknowledging that a vast number of other permutations could be explored.
elements.sent-2411-154: We examined the timing baseline duration for each pulsar (refer to Figure 4) to investigate any potential correlation with $\hat{A}$ and S/N as pulsars were sequentially removed from the timing array.
elements.sent-2411-155: Our analysis revealed no apparent correlation.
elements.sent-2411-156: However, removing pulsars can decrease the time spanned by a PTA's constituent observations.
elements.sent-2411-157: This consequently affects the frequency range over which the PTA can detect a signal.
elements.sent-2411-158: In this analysis, we maintain a constant number of frequency components in the gravitational wave background (GWB) model.
elements.sent-2411-159: This decision is justified by the fact that, as pulsars are removed in the specific sequence chosen for this work, the observation span decreases by less than 60 days.
elements.sent-2411-160: This reduction is minimal compared to the total timespan of 16 years for the complete 67-pulsar PTA.
elements.sent-2411-161: Dropout analysis \citep{2019ApJ...880..116A, 2020ApJ...905L..34A was employed in NG15 to evaluate the significance of the GWB signal in individual pulsars.}
elements.sent-2411-162: The dropout method introduces a binary parameter to either include or exclude the common signal for a specific pulsar.
elements.sent-2411-163: The dropout factor or the Bayes factor then indicates the degree to which each pulsar supports the presence of the GWB signal.
elements.sent-2411-164: In our approach, instead of evaluating each pulsar independently we removed pulsars in a cumulative manner.
elements.sent-2411-165: While both methods aim to understand the robustness and consistency of the detected signal, the dropout method focuses on the individual contribution of each pulsar in isolation.
elements.sent-2411-166: In contrast, our cumulative removal approach provides insight into the collective impact on the signal as pulsars are removed and so no single removal (other than the first) can be viewed in isolation.

elements.sent-2411-172: We use the OS in this analysis because it is a well established statistic which was widely used in NG15, allowing us to make direct comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-173: Additionally, the techniques we employed are computationally inexpensive and so enabled us to calculate the recovered signal strength thousands of times within a reasonable time frame.

elements.sent-2411-177: We acknowledge that we have selected to investigate only certain outstanding features that result from our analysis, specifically those which seem to contradict the null hypothesis that the NG15 analysis is comprehensively modeled and internally consistent.

elements.sent-2411-180: We have investigated the behavior of recovered signal strength when pulsars are removed one by one from the NANOGrav 15-year data set.
elements.sent-2411-181: Despite variations during pulsar removal that we had not anticipated, these fluctuations are consistent with those observed in simulated data sets that have matching noise properties and a gravitational wave background injected.
elements.sent-2411-182: After calculating $p$-values for the data we find that the results are consistent with the simulated data sets and, therefore, with a stochastic gravitational wave background signal.
elements.sent-2411-183: The purpose of our analysis was to provide internal consistency checks on the NANOGrav 15-year data set, and the results indicate that the data have successfully passed these tests.
elements.sent-2411-184: It also remains essential to conduct further investigations that may reveal systematics or unmodeled physical effects within the data.

elements.sent-2411-216: Evidence has emerged for a stochastic signal correlated among 67 pulsars within the 15 yr pulsar-timing data set compiled by the NANOGrav collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-217: Similar signals have been found in data from the European, Indian, Parkes, and Chinese pulsar timing arrays.
elements.sent-2411-218: This signal has been interpreted as indicative of the presence of a nanohertz stochastic gravitational-wave background (GWB).
elements.sent-2411-219: To explore the internal consistency of this result, we investigate how the recovered signal strength changes as we remove the pulsars one by one from the data set.
elements.sent-2411-220: We calculate the signal strength using the (noise-marginalized) optimal statistic, a frequentist metric designed to measure the correlated excess power in the residuals of the arrival times of the radio pulses.
elements.sent-2411-221: After conducting identical analyses on simulated data sets, we do not find anything inconsistent with the presence of a stochastic GWB in the NANOGrav 15 yr data.
elements.sent-2411-222: It is expected that inspiralling supermassive black holes binaries (SMBHB), with masses in the range $\sim$$£10^8 \,--\, 10^{10}\,M_\odot$ and redshifts $z \lesssim 2$, will produce a stochastic GW background (SGWB) in this frequency band \citep{2003ApJ...583..616J, 2004ApJ...611..623S, 2008MNRAS.390..192S, 2014ApJ...789..156M , although individual sources and cosmological backgrounds may also be present with comparable amplitudes.}
elements.sent-2411-223: These results came from analysing the NANOGrav 15 yr data set \citep[NG15;][]{2023ApJ...951L...8A , the second data release from the European PTA \citep[EPTA2;][]{2023A A...678A..50E which includes the first data release from the Indian PTA \citep[][]{2018JApA...39...51J, 2023A A...678A..50E , the third data release from the Parkes PTA \citep[][]{2023ApJ...951L...6R and the first data release from the Chinese PTA \cite[][]{2023RAA....23g5024X .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: In the near future, increased sensitivity is expected to be achieved using the forthcoming third data release from the International PTA which will combine many of the aforementioned data sets, data from new observatories such as MeerKAT \citep[which has already made its first data release, MPTA1;][]{2023MNRAS.519.3976M and eventually the Square Kilometre Array \citep[SKA;][]{2015aska.confE..37J .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: In this paper we investigate the NANOGrav 15 yr data set (NG15 hereafter).
elements.sent-2411-226: The NANOGrav collaboration recently published evidence for an SGWB in the 15 yr data set \citep[NG15;][]{2023ApJ...951L...8A .}
elements.sent-2411-227: Because our analysis requires the signal strength to be calculated thousands of times, we employ the noise marginalized (NM) optimal statistic (OS), a computationally inexpensive frequentist detection metric, in our assessment of any signal.
elements.sent-2411-228: As well as a signal with the HD spatial correlations expected of an SGWB, we also searched for signals with monopole and dipole spatial correlations.
elements.sent-2411-229: In order to quantify how unusual these features were, we designed metrics to quantify them and then compared the data to 100 realizations of simulated PTAs with an SGWB injected and with noise properties based on NG15.
elements.sent-2411-230: We find that the results of our pulsar removal analysis of NG15 are consistent with the simulated data sets and, therefore, with an SGWB signal.
elements.sent-2411-231: When using PTAs to search for an SGWB, multiple timing noise sources vary over timescales similar to the GW signal (e.g., intrinsic pulsar noise, interstellar-medium–induced radio-frequency-dependent fluctuations, and timing-model errors).
elements.sent-2411-232: Therefore, a useful complement to the Bayesian framework is the computationally inexpensive frequentist detection metric known as the OS \citep[][]{PhysRevD.79.084030 .}
elements.sent-2411-233: When looking for a stochastic signal process that is spatially correlated between different pulsars, the cross-covariance between two pulsars ($a\neq b$) is used; \begin{equation}\label{eq:residuals} \rho_{ab} = \left< \delta t_a \delta t_b \right>.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-234: The optimal amplitude statistic, $\hat{A}$, is defined as the value of the amplitude parameter $A$ that minimizes the quantity \begin{equation}\label{eq:OS} \chi^2 = \sum_{a, b<a} \left(\frac{\rho_{ab}-A^2\Gamma_{ab}}{\sigma_{ab}}\right)^2\,,\end{equation} where $\sigma_{ab}$ are the uncertainties in the cross-correlations and where $\Gamma_{ab}$ is the overlap reduction function (ORF; which quantifies the degree of correlation between pulsars as a function of their positions on the sky.) Under the assumptions that the noise is Gaussian and is much greater than the signal it can be shown that this statistic is optimal in the sense that it maximizes the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N).
elements.sent-2411-235: However, in addition to the HD spatial correlations induced by an SGWB, other sources of spatially correlated timing residuals are possible.
elements.sent-2411-236: The ORFs for these spatial correlations are respectively \begin{equation}\Gamma^{\rm Monopole}_{ab} = 1, \label{eq:monopoleORF} \Gamma^{\rm Dipole}_{ab} = \cos\xi_{ab},\quad and \label{eq:dipoleORF} \Gamma^{\rm HD}_{ab} = \frac{\delta_{ab}}{2}+\frac{1}{2}-\frac{(1-\cos\xi_{ab})}{4}\bigg(\frac{1}{2}\nonumber -3\log\left[\frac{1-\cos\xi_{ab}}{2}\right]\bigg) \label{eq:HDORF},\end{equation} where $\xi_{ab}$ is the angle on the sky between pulsar $a$ and pulsar $b$.
elements.sent-2411-237: In this paper, both the single component (SC) OS (see Eq.~\ref{eq:OS ) and MCOS (see Eq.~\ref{eq:MCOS ) are used.}}
elements.sent-2411-238: SCOS analyses are performed one at a time using each of the three ORFs in Eqs.~\ref{eq:monopoleORF to \ref{eq:HDORF .}}
elements.sent-2411-239: The OS and the MCOS are computationally cheap to evaluate (for a typical PTA data set they can be evaluated in $\approx 0.5 \; \mathrm{s )$.}
elements.sent-2411-240: This characteristic makes them ideal for iterative analyses and facilitates the comprehensive exploration of a range of scenarios, such as different combinations of pulsars and spatial correlations and also when marginalizing over pulsar-noise parameters (see Section \ref{subsec:nmos ).}
elements.sent-2411-241: In cases where pulsars exhibit significant red noise, the OS yields biased results due to the strong covariance between individual red-noise parameters and the amplitude of the SGWB.
elements.sent-2411-242: This bias can be mitigated by marginalizing over the individual pulsars’ red-noise parameters using the posterior probability distributions from a Bayesian analysis of all PTA pulsars.
elements.sent-2411-243: This Bayesian step includes an SGWB red-noise model that has a spectrum common to all pulsars, but that is agnostic regarding any spatial correlations between pulsars.
elements.sent-2411-244: In practice, each time the OS is calculated, the pulsars' individual red-noise parameters are drawn from the posterior distributions of the spatially uncorrelated Bayesian analysis; we calculate the OS 10,000 times, resulting in a distribution from which an average OS can be calculated.
elements.sent-2411-245: We, therefore, use the NMOS throughout this work.
elements.sent-2411-246: The amplitude of the SGWB spectrum is left as a free parameter and we assume a log-uniform prior probability distribution between $10^{-18}$ and $10^{-11}$.
elements.sent-2411-247: We assume a log-uniform prior probability distribution between $10^{-20}$ and $10^{-11}$ for the amplitude of the red noise and a uniform prior probability distribution between 0 and 7 for its slope.
elements.sent-2411-248: The red-noise parameters of each pulsar subarray are then drawn from the Bayesian posterior distributions as described in Section \ref{subsec:nmos .}
elements.sent-2411-249: Our aim is to use the above methods to quickly search for a correlated signal in the timing residuals of a PTA.
elements.sent-2411-250: The order in which we removed pulsars is based on their noise levels, specifically the weighted root-mean-square of epoch-averaged post-fit timing residuals after whitening (i.e., any red-noise contribution is removed) as listed in Table 6 of \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...9A}\footnote{For PSR~B1937+21, the value listed in Table 6 of \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...9A is incorrect.}}
elements.sent-2411-251: As a result, PSR~B1937+21 appears at a position in the pulsar removal ordering that reflects the value quoted in the \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...9A table.}
elements.sent-2411-252: We implemented two distinct exclusion methodologies for the NG15 pulsars: one where the least noisy pulsars were excluded first, and another where the most noisy pulsars were excluded first.
elements.sent-2411-253: A priori we anticipate that the removal of the least noisy pulsars will have the most pronounced impact on the signals recovered from the timing residuals.
elements.sent-2411-254: It is for this reason that we prioritize the scenario in which the least noisy pulsars are removed first.
elements.sent-2411-255: The case where we remove the most noisy pulsars first is discussed in Appendix \ref{app:noisy_first of this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-256: The results from both the single-component SCNMOS and the MCNMOS analyses are shown in Table~\ref{tab:allpulsars and Figure~\ref{fig:full_array_stats , and were calculated for monopole, dipole and HD spatial correlations.}}
elements.sent-2411-257: As we are using the full array in this case, this also permits us to compare our results with the fully Bayesian analysis that includes HD spatial correlations in the \citet[][]{2023ApJ...951L...8A paper.}

elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/best_removed_first_sc.png} \caption{\label{fig:NG15_SC_bestfirst} The evolution of the SCNMOS squared amplitude $\hat{A}^2$ and its S/N for NG15 as the least noisy pulsars are removed one by one from the PTA.
